content.assessment: Targeted specialist workforce expansion most directly addresses documented spatial inequity: the stark regional concentration of specialist positions (45 in Budapest versus 2-3 in rural Transdanubian counties) is a textbook access-inequality finding (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05), compounded by the strain integrated mainstream schools already report in serving the growing SEN population (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06). This proposal has the clearest and most direct mobility-relevant mechanism among the six, since it targets whether identified children actually receive services rather than merely being labeled. Its main equity risk is that rising formal enrollment in training programs is not a reliable proxy for future practicing supply, given that many trainees are parents unlikely to enter the workforce.
opportunities: Traveling specialist-network models could specifically reach underserved rural/northeastern areas currently facing near-total workforce absence
Monitoring of habilitation/therapy hour delivery could reveal previously invisible access gaps among already-identified students
strengths: Directly targets the sharpest documented spatial inequality in specialist access
Addresses the gap between identification and actual service delivery, which affects real educational outcomes rather than just classification
Responds to evidence that institutional capacity, not just student characteristics, shapes integration success
threats: If retention incentives are not carefully targeted, expansion could still concentrate in already better-served urban areas
Broader teacher salary reforms not specifically targeted at specialists may fail to close the practicing workforce gap
weaknesses: Rising training enrollment does not reliably translate into practicing workforce, undermining medium-term expectations for supply growth
Without co-targeting of financing and gatekeeping reform, specialist expansion alone may not resolve exclusion mechanisms embedded in classification and admissions
